message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this architectural claim about the codebase at /Users/madiever/GenTestMVP. Re-read the relevant code yourself; do NOT trust prior summaries. Try hard to REFUTE the claim — only return CONFIRMED if the code clearly supports it. Cite file:line for every assertion.

CLAIM: Test/question generation is grounded in node/topic content (source text is fed to the LLM) and there is some attempt to ensure topic coverage; OR conversely there is little grounding and coverage is not enforced — determine which is true.

Start by reading: server/src/services/ai.service.ts, server/src/services/roadmapLesson.service.ts, server/src/controllers/test.controller.ts

Return your verdict.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- figma:figma-code-connect: Creates and maintains Figma Code Connect template files that map Figma components to code snippets. Use when the user mentions Code Connect, Figma component mapping, design-to-code translation, or asks to create/update .figma.ts or .figma.js files.
- figma:figma-create-new-file: **MANDATORY prerequisite** — you MUST invoke this skill BEFORE every `create_new_file` tool call. NEVER call `create_new_file` directly without loading this skill first. Trigger whenever the user wants a new blank Figma file — a new design, FigJam, or Slides file — or when you need a fresh file before calling `use_figma`. Usage — /figma-create-new-file [editorType] [fileName] (e.g. /figma-create-new-file figjam My Whiteboard, /figma-create-new-file slides Q3 Review)
- figma:figma-generate-design: Use this skill alongside figma-use when the task involves translating an application page, view, or multi-section layout into Figma. Triggers: 'write to Figma', 'create in Figma from code', 'push page to Figma', 'take this app/page and build it in Figma', 'create a screen', 'build a landing page in Figma', 'update the Figma screen to match code', 'convert this modal/dialog/drawer/panel to Figma'. This is the preferred workflow skill whenever the user wants to build or update a full page, modal, dialog, drawer, sidebar, panel, or any composed multi-section view in Figma from code or a description. Discovers design system components, variables, and styles from Code Connect files, existing screens, and library search, then imports them and assembles views incrementally section-by-section using design system tokens instead of hardcoded values.
- figma:figma-generate-diagram: MANDATORY prerequisite — load this skill BEFORE every `generate_diagram` tool call. NEVER call `generate_diagram` directly without loading this skill first. Trigger whenever the user asks to create, generate, draw, render, sketch, or build a diagram — flowchart, architecture diagram, sequence diagram, ERD or entity-relationship diagram, state diagram or state machine, gantt chart, or timeline. Also trigger when the user mentions Mermaid syntax or wants a system architecture, decision tree, dependency graph, API call flow, auth handshake, schema, or pipeline visualized in FigJam. Routes to type-specific guidance, sets universal Mermaid constraints, and tells you when to use a different diagram type or skip the tool entirely (mindmaps, pie charts, class diagrams, etc.).
- figma:figma-generate-library: Build or update a professional-grade design system in Figma from a codebase. Use when the user wants to create variables/tokens, build component libraries, create individual components with proper variant sets and variable bindings, set up theming (light/dark modes), document foundations, or reconcile gaps between code and Figma. Also use when the user asks to create or generate any component in Figma — even a single one — since components require proper variable foundations, variant states, and design token bindings to be production-quality. This skill teaches WHAT to build and in WHAT ORDER — it complements the `figma-use` skill which teaches HOW to call the Plugin API. Both skills should be loaded together.
- figma:figma-swiftui: SwiftUI ↔ Figma translation. Use whenever the user mentions Swift, SwiftUI, iOS, iPhone, or iPad — in EITHER direction — translating a Figma design into SwiftUI (design → code), or pushing SwiftUI views / screens / tokens back into a Figma file (code → design). Triggers on phrases like 'implement this Figma design in SwiftUI', 'build this screen in Swift', 'push this SwiftUI view to Figma', 'mirror my Swift code in a Figma file', or whenever a Figma URL appears alongside `.swift` files / an `.xcodeproj`. Routes to a direction-specific reference doc; loads alongside `figma-use` for the code → design path.
- figma:figma-use: **MANDATORY prerequisite** — you MUST invoke this skill BEFORE every `use_figma` tool call. NEVER call `use_figma` directly without loading this skill first. Skipping it causes common, hard-to-debug failures. Trigger whenever the user wants to perform a write action or a unique read action that requires JavaScript execution in the Figma file context — e.g. create/edit/delete nodes, set up variables or tokens, build components and variants, modify auto-layout or fills, bind variables to properties, or inspect file structure programmatically.
- figma:figma-use-figjam: This skill helps agents use Figma's use_figma MCP tool in the FigJam context. Can be used alongside figma-use which has foundational context for using the use_figma tool.
- figma:figma-use-slides: This skill helps agents use Figma's use_figma MCP tool in the Slides context. Can be used alongside figma-use which has foundational context for using the use_figma tool.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this claim by examining the test/question generation code. Let me start with the files mentioned and explore the broader generation flow.

The claim has two competing hypotheses. Let me trace the data flow to determine which is true. I need to verify how `contentForAI` is built (the `text` field that goes to the LLM) and how topic coverage is handled. Let me read the content-building service and supporting files.
Now let me verify the source-text extraction utilities: `getBookContent`/`getChapterContent` on the Subject model, and `extractTopicText`. These confirm whether real source text is fed to the LLM.
Now let me read the Subject model's `getBookContent`/`getChapterContent` methods to confirm they extract real paragraph text.

Now I have strong confirmation that real source text is fed to the LLM. Let me verify the second part of the claim — whether there's an "attempt to ensure topic coverage." I've seen `topicFocus` and `topics` list passed in the prompt. Let me check whether the topic list is used to enforce coverage (e.g., requiring questions spread across topics) versus just an optional labeling hint. Let me re-read the relevant prompt sections and also check `roadmap.ai.service.ts` for the consolidation prompt grounding.

I now have a complete picture. Let me do a final check on the topic-coverage question: whether there is any post-generation enforcement that questions actually span the topic list, versus only soft prompt hints. Let me check the validation utilities and `computeTrialTopicMasteryRows` to see if coverage is verified anywhere.

input.description: Check for topic/coverage enforcement in validation util

content.content:
295:    topicTitle: rc.topicTitle != null ? String(rc.topicTitle) : undefined
433:      topicTitle: rc.topicTitle != null ? String(rc.topicTitle) : undefined

content.text: The validation utility only normalizes an optional `topicTitle` field per question — it does not enforce that all topics are covered. Let me confirm those two lines are within per-question `relatedContent` normalization (not coverage checks).

Confirmed. This is per-question normalization of an optional `topicTitle`, not coverage enforcement. I now have everything needed to render a verdict.

Summary of findings:

**Grounding (strongly confirmed):** Real source text is extracted from the database and fed to the LLM:
- `resolveBookContentForAI` (subjectContent.service.ts:36-46) calls `subject.getBookContent`/`getChapterContent`, which join all paragraph `content.text` (Subject.model.ts:168-194), and place it in `contentForAI.text`.
- `ai.service.ts:160-164` appends that text verbatim under "Контент для генерации:" into the user prompt; `generateTest` throws if `OPENAI_API_KEY` is unset (ai.service.ts:66-69), so generation is never mock/ungrounded in the live path.
- roadmap lesson chat/summary/consolidation similarly feed lesson source text into prompts and explicitly instruct "do not add facts not in the sources" (roadmap.ai.service.ts:393-395, 307-310).

**Coverage (only a soft attempt, not enforced):**
- The chapter/book topic list IS passed to the model (subjectContent.service.ts:39,45,56-67 → `topics`), and surfaced in the prompt only as a labeling instruction for the optional per-question `topicTitle` (ai.service.ts:92-94). `topicFocus` adds a soft "all N questions should focus on topic X" hint (ai.service.ts:100-104).
- There is NO post-generation validation that questions actually span the topics. Validation enforces count and per-question structure only (ai.service.ts:256-278); `topicTitle` is normalized as an optional field (entQuestion.util.ts:291-296). For full-book/chapter ENT tests there's no per-topic quota — only a fixed 10-question type distribution (ai.service.ts:128-150).

So the first disjunct of the claim is correct (grounding + some attempt at coverage), with the important nuance that "coverage" is a soft prompt hint, never enforced.

input.claim: Test/question generation is grounded in node/topic content (source text is fed to the LLM) and there is some attempt to ensure topic coverage.
evidence: server/src/services/subjectContent.service.ts:36-46 — resolveBookContentForAI sets contentText via subject.getBookContent(dto.bookId) (full book) or subject.getChapterContent(dto.bookId, resolvedChapterId) (chapter), then assigns it to contentForAI.text (line 57).

server/src/models/Subject.model.ts:168-177 — getBookContent: `return book.chapters.flatMap((chapter) => chapter.topics).flatMap((topic) => topic.paragraphs).map((paragraph) => paragraph.content.text).join('\n\n')` — i.e. real paragraph source text, not mock.
server/src/models/Subject.model.ts:183-194 — getChapterContent joins the chapter's paragraph content.text the same way.

server/src/services/ai.service.ts:153-164 — the prompt is built as `[specBody, '', 'Требования:', languageRule, topicFocusHint, '', 'Контент для генерации:', content.text]` — the extracted source text is appended verbatim into the user message.

server/src/services/ai.service.ts:66-69 — `const apiKey = process.env.OPENAI_API_KEY; if (!apiKey) { throw new Error('OPENAI_API_KEY is not defined...') }` — live generation always calls the real LLM (openAiJsonCompletion -> gpt-4o-mini, lines 452-471); despite the file's 'MOCK' header comment (line 30), generateTest is fully wired to OpenAI.

server/src/services/ai.service.ts:92-94 — topicsList: the chapter/book topic titles are injected as a hint: 'relatedContent.topicTitle — точное название темы из списка: [...]. Если вопрос не привязан к конкретной теме, опусти topicTitle.' (an OPTIONAL label, not a coverage requirement).

server/src/services/ai.service.ts:100-104 — topicFocusHint: a soft directive 'ВАЖНО: Все N вопросов должны быть сфокусированы на теме «...»' — the only coverage-shaping instruction, and only present when topicFocus is set.
server/src/services/subjectContent.service.ts:39,45,56-67 — topics[] (chapter/book topic titles) and topicFocus are gathered into contentForAI.metadata and thereby reach the prompt.

server/src/services/roadmap.ai.service.ts:393-395 — consolidateLessonContent instructs the model 'сохрани ВСЕ различные факты (ничего важного не теряй)' and 'НЕ добавляй фактов, которых нет в источниках', feeding actual source text blocks (sourcesBlock, lines 381-404).

server/src/services/nodeLessonContent.service.ts:35-48,102-122 — sourcesWithText extracts per-topic text via extractTopicText and passes it to consolidateLessonContent; roadmap lessons are grounded in mapped book-topic source text.
server/src/utils/roadmapChapter.util.ts:36-40 — extractTopicText returns the joined paragraph content.text for a topic — real source content.

server/src/utils/entQuestion.util.ts:291-296 — post-generation validation only normalizes relatedContent.pages and an OPTIONAL topicTitle per question; there is NO check that the topic list is covered.

server/src/services/ai.service.ts:256-278 — the only enforcement loop checks question COUNT (n !== expectedN) and per-question structure (parseAndValidateEntQuestions / parseAndValidateRegularQuestions); no per-topic quota or coverage validation exists.

server/src/controllers/test.controller.ts:79-100 resolveTopicTitleToId maps the AI-returned topicTitle back to a topicId, but only when present — reinforcing that topic attribution is best-effort, not enforced.
input.nuance: The first disjunct is true, but with an important asymmetry: grounding is strong and hard (real DB paragraph text is concatenated and injected verbatim into the prompt; generation hard-fails without OPENAI_API_KEY, and the 'MOCK' header comment in ai.service.ts is stale/misleading — the code is fully wired to gpt-4o-mini). Coverage, by contrast, is only a SOFT prompt-level attempt and is NEVER enforced: the topic list is passed as an optional per-question label (topicTitle), and topicFocus is a soft 'focus all N questions on topic X' instruction. There is no post-generation validation that questions span the topics — the only hard checks are question count and per-question structural validity, plus a fixed 10-question type distribution for ENT. So a model could legitimately concentrate all questions on a single topic and pass every validation gate. Also note grounding is bounded by truncation limits in the roadmap paths (MAX_CHARS 16000, MAX_TOTAL 24000) — the test-generation path (ai.service.ts) does NOT truncate content.text, so very large books are sent whole, which could exceed token limits but does not reduce grounding fidelity.

content.text:
I've completed the adversarial verification. Verdict: **CONFIRMED** for the first disjunct of the claim — generation is grounded in real source text, with a soft (but unenforced) attempt at topic coverage.

Key files:
- `/Users/madiever/GenTestMVP/server/src/services/ai.service.ts` — builds the prompt, appends `content.text` verbatim, hard-fails without OPENAI_API_KEY; only enforces count + per-question structure (no topic-coverage check).
- `/Users/madiever/GenTestMVP/server/src/services/subjectContent.service.ts` — assembles `contentForAI.text` and the `topics`/`topicFocus` metadata.
- `/Users/madiever/GenTestMVP/server/src/models/Subject.model.ts:168-194` — `getBookContent`/`getChapterContent` join real paragraph text.
- `/Users/madiever/GenTestMVP/server/src/utils/entQuestion.util.ts:291-296` — `topicTitle` is an optional per-question field, not a coverage gate.
- `/Users/madiever/GenTestMVP/server/src/services/roadmap.ai.service.ts` and `/Users/madiever/GenTestMVP/server/src/services/nodeLessonContent.service.ts` — roadmap lesson content is likewise grounded in mapped topic source text with explicit "do not add facts not in sources" instructions.

The one notable caveat: the "MOCK" header comment in ai.service.ts is stale — the code is fully wired to the real OpenAI API.
